subject: [swap tier discussion] Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm/zswap: Implement proactive writeback

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested renaming the memory tiering interface to a swap tiering interface, proposing 'memory.swap.tiers.max' as a configuration option and recommending support for only two values (0/max) initially, with plans to expand incrementally.
reviews.raw_body:
If we will have one interface for all the tiers for memory tiering,
I'd rather we do the swap for swap tiering. So maybe
memory.swap.tiers.max or memory.swap.tiered max?

The file can show the limits for all tiers when read, and maybe write
something like "echo 'tierX max' > memory.swap.tiers.max" to it to set
a new limit. We can support only 0/max for now to enable/disable
tiers. In the future, we can also allow something like "auto" to
automatically scale the limit based on the swapfile size and
memory.swap.max, similar to the direction memory tiering is heading
in.

I think we can start with just this interface for now, and expand
incrementally. For proactive zswap writeback, we can add
memory.swap.tiers.demote or something, and only support zswap
initially?

2026-06-12.patch_summary: This patch proposes a new interface for swap tiering, similar to zswap, which allows setting limits on memory usage. The initial implementation will support only two values: 0 and max, effectively mirroring the existing behavior. This change aims to provide more fine-grained control over swap tiering.

reviews.author: YoungJun Park
reviews.summary:
Reviewer YoungJun Park noted that implementing a per-tier maximum for zswap would require significant changes to the current interface, including modifying the internal logic and adding new configuration options. He suggested initially allowing only two values (0 and max) and integrating memory.zswap.max naturally into the new interface.

Reviewer noted that selecting zswap in memory.swap.tiers would not only disable writeback, but also block regular swap entirely, differing from the current semantic, and suggested retaining the interface to allow selective writeback by passing a desired tier into memory.zswap.writeback
reviews.raw_body:
Good idea, and it's certainly feasible. When I considered this a while
ago, the reasons I didn't take this direction were:

1. There's no real-world usage for adjusting the swap tier amount (it's
   either 0 or MAX). That said, your suggestion to initially allow only
   0 and max is the killing point, and it's making me reconsider.

2. The implementation cost seems high. The current implementation
   handles this at runtime via simple masking.

3. Relationship with swap.max:
   - If we tie it to the current interface, wouldn't limiting the swap
     amount within a selected tier already be possible? I wonder if
     that alone is enough.
   - If we add tier.max, it would need to be a subset of swap.max.
     (Any other complexities here?)

4. vswap enable/disable: vswap doesn't seem to have an amount-control
   aspect, so an on/off semantic would be clearer.
   https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/ai5kOOmR1LPTWs1J@yjaykim-PowerEdge-T330/T/#m8831ec057bf9387978d3bd698f51920600e09a04

In that case, the internal logic could stay roughly the same rather
than counting via a page counter. Something like:

1. Change the interface shell: tier.*.max \u2014 allow only 0 ~ max.
2. Keep the internal logic as is: 0 disables the mask (child memcgs
   off too), max enables it (child memcgs on too).
3. memory.zswap.max integrates naturally (it's memory."tier_name".max).
4. Extend later if use cases arise.

On balance I still lean toward the current interface, but if a per-tier
max is the better fit for memcg's direction and others feel the same,
I'm happy to switch. I'd like to hear Shakeel's thoughts again, and I'm
curious about others' opinions too.

A few more perspectives on the points below.

---

Let me clarify a part I wrote confusingly. Handling
memory.zswap.writeback via tiers is possible, but I don't think the
interface itself would be replaced even if memory.swap.tiers is adopted.

Selecting only zswap in memory.swap.tiers would not just disable
writeback.it would also block regular swap entirely, which differs
slightly from the current semantic. (... "Per the cgroup v2 docs: a
zswap-only tier setting is subtly different from setting
memory.swap.max to 0, since it still allows pages to be written to the
zswap pool; this has no effect if zswap is disabled, and swapping is
allowed unless memory.swap.max is set to 0.")

So the interface itself needs to be retained, and it could be extended
toward selective writeback \u2014 e.g., passing a desired tier into
memory.zswap.writeback so writeback targets only that tier. Currently
it only controls on/off. Other tiers probably don't need this. demotion
based on the selected tier should be enough.

Thanks,
Youngjun Park

2026-06-14.patch_summary: This patch proposes a new interface for swap tiering, similar to zswap, which allows setting limits on memory usage. The initial implementation will support only two values: 0 and max, effectively mirroring the existing behavior. This change aims to provide more fine-grained control over swap tiering.

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the patch does not implement a single interface for memory tiering, which is inconsistent with existing memory tiering practices and suggested aligning with this convention.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that a child swap tier should be able to be disabled independently of its parent, and suggested this would prevent the parent from being locked into using the child tier even if it's not desired.

Reviewer pointed out that the `memory.zswap.max` parameter is measured in compressed size, not uncompressed size, which could lead to incorrect swapping behavior.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed questioned the semantic difference between disabling zswap writeback and only enabling zswap as a tier, suggesting it's a minor distinction that may not impact practice due to zswap being rarely disabled at runtime.
reviews.raw_body:
What about a single interface as I suggested to remain consistent with
memory tiering?

---

I think a child should be able to disable a swap tier enabled by the
parent, but not vice versa.

---

Not really. memory.zswap.max is in terms of memory usage (compressed
size), not swap usage (uncompressed size).

[..]

---

I don't understand. How is disabling zswap writeback not equivalent to
only enabling zswap as a tier?

Do you just mean the fact that disabling zswap writeback is a noop of
zswap is disabled? It's a different interface so I think a small
semantic difference is okay. In practice, I doubt that zswap is being
disabled at runtime.

2026-06-15.patch_summary: This patch proposes a new interface for swap tiering, similar to zswap, which allows setting limits on memory usage. The initial implementation will support only two values: 0 and max, effectively mirroring the existing behavior. This change aims to provide more fine-grained control over swap tiering.

reviews.author: YoungJun Park
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that having static memory."tier_name".max files is unnatural given that swap tiers can be added or removed at runtime, and suggested a single interface like "swap.tiers.max" using a flat-keyed format.

Reviewer noted that the child's selected tiers should be a subset of the parent's, and that a child cannot enable a tier that the parent has disabled.

Reviewer noted that memory.zswap.max needs to be maintained separately and expressed uncertainty about its semantics, requesting further investigation.

Reviewer noted that there's a case where zswap_store() fails and pages fall back to the backing swap device, which introduces a semantic difference between 'zswap tier only' and 'zswap writeback disabled', making it difficult to unify them

reviewer suggested considering a zswap-like interface for swap tiers, allowing users to set memory limits and potentially adding more values in the future
reviews.raw_body:
Hello Yosry!

I agree. As I was implementing the interface for seeing feasibility
, I reconsidered it. Since swap tiers can be added or removed at runtime, 
having static memory."tier_name".max files seems unnatural.

A single interface like `swap.tiers.max` would be better. We can use a
flat-keyed format (similar to io.weight. same as you suggested)

echo ["tier_name"] ["0 or max"] > swap.tiers.max

I am now leaning towards this is a better direction than what I initially
suggested (memory.swap.tiers and memory.swap.tiers.effective).

Considering other reviews and Shakeel's reply, I will update my swap tier
patch accordingly.

---

Yes, we are on the same page. I missed a part in my explanation. I meant
that the child's selected tiers should be a subset of the parent's (which
is how the current swap tier suggestion works). 
A child cannot enable a tier that the parent has disabled.

---

I see, memory.zswap.max needs to be maintained separately. I will look
closer into its semantics. I might have misunderstood this part!

---

Isn't there a case where zswap_store() fails and pages fall back to the
backing swap device?

- "zswap tier only": Only zswap is allowed. Fallback to other swap is
  blocked.
- "zswap writeback disabled": zswap is allowed, but if zswap_store()
  fails, pages can still fall back to other swap devices.

Because of this slight semantic difference, I thought they couldn't be
fully unified. If my understanding is correct, we could extend the zswap
tier to select the target swap device for writeback, but replacing the
writeback interface entirely might be difficult.

---

I thought disabling zswap at runtime might have some use cases, but we
can discuss this further when we talk about the patch extending the
zswap tier.

Best regards,
Youngjun

reviews.author: YoungJun Park
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the 'zswap tier only' and 'zswap writeback disable' settings are equivalent, making them meaningless when combined, and suggested integrating or sharing underlying logic if the existing interface is to be kept.
reviews.raw_body:
Upon double-checking the code, my previous clarification was wrong.
You are right. Sorry for the confusion. "zswap tier only" is indeed
equivalent to "zswap writeback disabled".
(I'm not sure why I read the code that way...)

As I initially thought, it might be possible to replace the zswap writeback
control with the tiering mechanism.

If we need to keep the existing interface, we can integrate or share the
underlying logic (though the specific details need more thought anyway).

It can be summarized as follows:

- "zswap tier only" + "zswap writeback disable" -> meaningless (noop)
- "zswap tier only" + "zswap writeback enable" -> meaningless (no writabck backend exist)
- "zswap tier with other tiers" + "zswap writeback disable" -> uses only zswap
  (can be replaced by "zswap tier only". This code could be intergrated, modified or something.)
- "zswap tier with other tiers" + "zswap writeback enable" -> works as is

As mentioned in the previous email, the zswap tier on/off control comes as a
bonus (though, as you pointed out, we may need to discuss if it's actually
needed).

BR,
Youngjun

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary: Author agrees with YoungJun Park's suggestion of using a zswap-like interface for swap tiers and proposes adopting Yosry's proposal instead.
reviews.raw_body:
I like Yosry's proposal. Let's go with that.

(I am travelling, so will be slow to respond)

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that enabling swap tiering and memory.zswap.writeback simultaneously could be problematic, suggesting disabling writeback when tiering is enabled or making it dependent on a config option to mitigate potential issues.
sentiment_signals: concern about having both interfaces active at the same time
reviews.raw_body:
Hmm we might want to somehow disable memory.zswap.writeback if tiering
is enabled, to avoid having to deal with this. But I am not sure how
possible this is.

If swap tiering is behind a config option, maybe we can disable
memory.zswap.writeback under that config option? But then if distros
start enabling the config option it might break some users. Not sure
what's the right way to do this, but having both interfaces active at
the same time is annoying.

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Nhat Pham noted that allowing zswap to be set as its own tier without vswap is meaningless and suggested making it a no-op, instead warning users about the invalid configuration.

Reviewer noted that even without vswap, a cgroup interface is needed to prescribe allocation and usage of swap slots, including prohibiting IO operations on those slots
reviews.raw_body:
TBH, without vswap, we should not allow setting zswap as its own tier.
It's meaningless. Maybe makes it a no-op, and warn users what they're
setting is gibberish?

---

With tiering and without vswap, you still need an interface to
prescribe that a cgroup can:

1. Allocate slots from a certain swap device.

2. Use zswap, backed by those slots.

3. But no IO is allowed to those slots (no writeback or fallback on
zswap failure).

So we still need memory.zswap.writeback, until we get rid of non-vswap
case for zswap.

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham questioned the logic of setting zswap as its own tier without vswap, pointing out that there would be no phys swap device to allocate from for the backing slot, and suggested that using vswap makes more sense
reviews.raw_body:
Without vswap, if you set zswap as its own tier, which phys swap
device should we allocate from for the backing slot? :)

With vswap then it makes sense (and would probably be the "default"
for zswap setup until we enable zswap writeback).

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary: reviewer questioned whether the current implementation of allocating a swap slot in a swapfile during reclaim would work with swap tiering, specifically expressing concern about how the allocated swap slot respects the allowed swap tiers
sentiment_signals: requested clarification on swap tiering compatibility
reviews.raw_body:
Today we just allocate a swap slot in a swapfile during reclaim,
before swapout, and zswap will just writeback to that one. I assume
the same will work with swap tiering, except that maybe the way that
swap slot will respect the allowed swap tiers?

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary: Reviewer questioned whether setting zswap as the only tier would allow allocation of a swap slot in a swap file, and asked for clarification on how this scenario is handled
reviews.raw_body:
Yep! So if we set zswap as the only tier, then it wouldn't be able to
allocate a swap slot in swapfile right?

Or are you suggesting that if we set zswap as the only tier then we
can allocate from any swapfile (since we're not doing any IO anyway)?

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed pointed out that the patch description's implication of zswap being the only tier is incorrect, and suggested rephrasing to accurately reflect this.

Reviewer noted that having zswap as the only swap tier would be equivalent to disabling writeback, but since memcg cannot use swap slots from any swapfile in this case, zswap is unusable; they suggested enabling vswap to allow making zswap the only tier.
reviews.raw_body:
Ohh I thought you meant we shouldn't allow zswap to be a tier at all,
not the *only* tier.

---

Hmm, technically having zswap as the only tier should be equivalent to
disabling writeback, but you're right that if zswap is the only tier
than the memcg is not allowed to use swap slots from any swapfile, so
zswap cannot be used. Very good point :)

In this case I think yes, we need vswap to be enabled to allow making
zswap the only tier. That's one gap between zswap being the only tier
and disabling zswap writeback, the former requires vswap while the
latter doesn't.

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch proposes a new interface for swap tiering, similar to zswap, which allows setting limits on memory usage. The initial implementation will support only two values: 0 and max, effectively mirroring the existing behavior. This change aims to provide more fine-grained control over swap tiering.

reviews.author: Nhat Pham
reviews.summary: Reviewer expressed frustration with the coupling of swap/zswap, but noted that vswap would simplify provisioning for in-memory compressed swap, which is a positive aspect.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah the coupling of swap/zswap makes reasoning about these kinds of
things so annoying. :)

If anything, with vswap, I'll stop having to explain to folks why they
have to provision on-disk swapfile when they only want to use
in-memory compressed swap, and that's a win in my book.

2026-06-17.patch_summary: This patch proposes a new interface for swap tiering, similar to zswap, which allows setting limits on memory usage. The initial implementation will support only two values: 0 and max, effectively mirroring the existing behavior. This change aims to provide more fine-grained control over swap tiering.
